text: The surgeon creates a tunnel beneath the lesser sac along the lesser curve, making an incision at the angle of His. The posterior fundus is lifted and stapled to form a square pouch, followed by the dissection of the omentum.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and creates a loop of intestine with the biliopancreatic limb, positioning it on the right side of the screen to ensure proper tension towards the gastric pouch.

The liver retractor is seen falling down, but the surgeon proceeds without adjusting it. The straight line of the gastric pouch is sewn to the underside of the biliopancreatic limb, which is positioned on the right side of the screen. The staple line is run to the back side of the loop of bowel to maintain tension.
The surgeon grabs the loop of intestine, with the assistant holding it steady, and ties a surgeon's knot to secure the loop in place.

The surgeon creates a two and a half centimeter anastomosis on the anti-mesenteric side, using a fenestrated bipolar instrument as a right angle to mirror the incision. The incision is made full thickness, with a lip left for sewing. The surgeon then performs a hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy, starting with full thickness bites using vicryl sutures at the center and sewing around the incision.

The surgeon places the second stitch, running the left stitch to the right and the right stitch to the left, ensuring no hole remains at the site where the stitches meet. The needle is loaded and used to lock the corners, with a technique of lifting up and pushing down to set the stitch. A forty French visagee is sent down to check for narrowing of the loop, and the process is repeated on the opposite side, crossing the stitches.
The surgeon secures the nest muscles using four or five interrupted Lembert sutures to relieve tension, ensuring the inclusion of both the outflow and inflow areas to prevent leakage.

The surgeon performs a series of Lembert sutures to relieve tension on the full thickness closure. A slip knot is created by forming a C loop with the limb on the screen's right side, using a fenestrated bipolar to tack it. The surgeon switches hands to create a square knot, lifting the needle and sliding the knot down to maintain tension. Additional sutures are placed, with the tail being tacked using the fenestrated bipolar, ensuring tension is maintained and the knot is locked securely.

The surgeon releases tension from the tissue using Lembert sutures, then creates a tunnel beneath the nearest vessel. A stapler is introduced to staple off the vessel, and the surgeon traces the tissue folds, taking four or five bites with the muscle sealer towards the root.

The surgeon runs the Roux limb of intestine to the right upper quadrant, bringing the jejunal loop or biliopancreatic limb up in a candy cane orientation. The biliopancreatic limb appears venous congested, but maintains reasonable blood flow.

The surgeon uses ICD-Green dye to assess blood flow in the biliopancreatic limb, noting that despite imperfect outflow, the limb has reasonable perfusion. The surgeon proceeds with creating a one to one and a half centimeter anastomosis for biliopancreatic fluid passage, ensuring the opening is appropriately sized for its function.

The surgeon creates a one to one and a half centimeter opening in the elementary limb for the anastomosis, using two vicryl sutures to sew around the site. The sutures are cut to ten inches, and the JJ or GJ anastomosis is slightly larger at twelve inches. The needle is loaded and controlled throughout the procedure.

The surgeon performs a conversion stitch, allowing for forehand and backhand stitching across the mesenteric defect. The assistant uses the assistant port to hold the left-side limb loop, preventing it from folding over and obscuring the surgical field. The mesenteric defect is pulled up, and the surgeon stitches in front and behind it to complete the Roux-Y configuration.
